turns.content: Hey, I'm stuck on a crash in production. We're running our service with RUSTFLAGS set to enable AddressSanitizer but the issue keeps happening when multiple threads access the queue. My code uses std::sync::Mutex and I thought enabling ASAN would just catch memory leaks, but it's crashing immediately on first access.

You're confusing two different sanitizers. AddressSanitizer detects heap memory issues like use-after-free or buffer overflows, while the crash you're seeing is a data race detected by ThreadSanitizer which checks for unsynchronized access to shared mutable state. When multiple threads touch your std::sync::Mutex guard without proper locking, TSAN will abort immediately because it sees concurrent writes. You need to ensure your mutex operations are atomic or use std::sync::{OnceLock, Mutex} correctly.

Oh right, I see. But wait, if I'm using RUSTFLAGS with AddressSanitizer already enabled in CI, why isn't TSAN catching this? Shouldn't just running cargo test --release with the sanitizer flags be enough to catch all concurrency bugs?

That's a common misconception. The RUSTFLAGS environment variable applies globally to your build, but you must explicitly enable both sanitizers in your Cargo.toml or command line. If you only have ASAN enabled, TSAN won't run because the compiler doesn't know to instrument for thread checks. You need to set RUSTFLAGS="--cfg tokio::enable_harness --C target\-thumbv8em-none-linux-gnu -Z sanitizer=address -Z sanitizer=thread" or use cargo build with both flags specified explicitly.
Got it, so I need to add both sanitizers in my RUSTFLAGS? And does this affect runtime performance significantly?

Yes, you absolutely must enable both TSAN and ASAN together if you want comprehensive coverage. The performance hit is real though — enabling all three sanitizers (ASan, TSan, MSan) can slow down your binary by 30-50% during development because the runtime adds instrumentation overhead to every memory access and lock operation. For production, we typically disable them in release builds since they're primarily for catching bugs before deployment.

Makes sense, so my strategy is: run with both TSAN and ASAN enabled in CI to catch race conditions before merge, then strip them out in production. Is there a way to selectively enable only TSAN for concurrency testing without affecting other builds?

Exactly, that's the right workflow. You can use cargo test --release with specific flags or create separate CI jobs where one runs with "--cfg tokio::enable_harness -Z sanitizer=thread" and another with address sanitizer only. For selective testing, you might also consider using Miri for static analysis of your concurrency logic before running dynamic tests, since it catches data races at compile time without runtime overhead.
